title: Prompt Engineering Terms and Acronym Learning Map
purpose: Structured JSON for creating individual lessons on prompt ingredients and the acronyms built from them.
sourceSummary: Based on the staged prompt-ingredient progression supplied by the user.

stages.description: This is the make your prompt clear stage.
stages.whyThisStage: Students first learn how to ask clearly by defining the task, goal, context, audience, tone, format, and result.

terms.meaning: The specific thing you want ChatGPT to do
The background ChatGPT needs before answering
How the answer should be arranged
The finished thing you want back

acronymDetails.teachingUse: Use this acronym as a lesson-ready prompt template for the stage shown. Each expansion word can become a prompt field or mini-lesson.
Use this acronym as a lesson-ready prompt template for the stage shown. Each expansion word can become a prompt field or mini-lesson.
Use this acronym as a lesson-ready prompt template for the stage shown. Each expansion word can become a prompt field or mini-lesson.

stages.description: Once students can ask clearly, they learn how to control the answer.
stages.whyThisStage: These ingredients make prompts more reliable, creative, and reusable. Students learn that ChatGPT performs better when it has boundaries, examples, and a clear role.

terms.meaning: The identity or perspective ChatGPT should use
The boundaries ChatGPT must stay within
What ChatGPT should avoid or be careful about
Samples that show what good looks like

acronymDetails.teachingUse: Use this acronym as a lesson-ready prompt template for the stage shown. Each expansion word can become a prompt field or mini-lesson.

Use this acronym as a lesson-ready prompt template for the stage shown. Each expansion word can become a prompt field or mini-lesson.

stages.description: This is where students move from asking ChatGPT anything to using specific content.
stages.whyThisStage: Students are now ready to upload documents, paste transcripts, summarize notes, transform messy content, and ask ChatGPT to follow a process.

terms.meaning: Breaking large content into smaller pieces

acronymDetails.teachingUse: Use this acronym as a lesson-ready prompt template for the stage shown. Each expansion word can become a prompt field or mini-lesson.
Use this acronym as a lesson-ready prompt template for the stage shown. Each expansion word can become a prompt field or mini-lesson.

Use this acronym as a lesson-ready prompt template for the stage shown. Each expansion word can become a prompt field or mini-lesson.

stages.description: This is where prompting becomes interactive.
stages.whyThisStage: Students can design prompts that teach, quiz, coach, simulate interviews, create roleplays, and respond to a learner’s needs.

terms.meaning: Testing or rehearsing with the user
The problem the user is trying to solve

acronymDetails.teachingUse: Use this acronym as a lesson-ready prompt template for the stage shown. Each expansion word can become a prompt field or mini-lesson.

Use this acronym as a lesson-ready prompt template for the stage shown. Each expansion word can become a prompt field or mini-lesson.

stages.description: This is where students learn that good prompting is iterative.
stages.whyThisStage: Students should stop expecting the first answer to be perfect. This stage teaches them to review, score, improve, and reprompt.

terms.meaning: Judging the quality of an answer

acronymDetails.teachingUse: Use this acronym as a lesson-ready prompt template for the stage shown. Each expansion word can become a prompt field or mini-lesson.

stages.description: This is where ChatGPT becomes a thinking partner.
stages.whyThisStage: Students can ask ChatGPT to compare options, expose assumptions, view a problem from multiple angles, and make recommendations.

terms.meaning: Looking at something from a different viewpoint

acronymDetails.teachingUse: Use this acronym as a lesson-ready prompt template for the stage shown. Each expansion word can become a prompt field or mini-lesson.

stages.description: This is the advanced quality-control layer.
stages.whyThisStage: These are advanced because they require students to understand that AI can sound confident while being wrong. They learn to demand evidence, challenge assumptions, and ask for confidence levels.
